text: Strength training reduces sports injuries to less than one-third of baseline risk (RR 0.315, 95% CI 0.207-0.480).
Stretching has no statistically significant effect on sports injury prevention (RR 0.963, 95% CI 0.846-1.095).
Proprioception training reduces sports injury risk by approximately 45% (RR 0.550, 95% CI 0.347-0.869).
Multi-component exercise programmes (combining strength, proprioception, and other modalities) reduce sports injury risk by approximately 35% (RR 0.655, 95% CI 0.520-0.826).
Overuse injuries can be reduced by approximately 47% through physical activity exercise programmes (RR 0.527, 95% CI 0.373-0.746).
Acute injuries can be reduced by approximately 35% through physical activity exercise programmes (RR 0.647, 95% CI 0.502-0.836).
Among injury prevention strategies, strength training shows the largest effect, followed by proprioception training, then multi-component programmes, while stretching alone shows no benefit.
